text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy for large myoma. This patient has a large myoma in the lower posterior right wall of the uterus and it is an intramural myoma.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic myomectomy by entering through Palmer's point. The camera port is positioned epigastrically, with additional ports placed in the lumbar and contralateral regions following the baseball diamond concept. A small stab wound is made in the right hypochondriac region using an #11 blade.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle towards the stomach, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm entry into the abdominal cavity. The abdominal wall is lifted, and the insufflator tubing is attached, with the preset pressure set to eighteen and flow rate at one liter per minute. As insufflation progresses, the actual pressure reaches the preset pressure, and the Veress needle is removed. The incision is enlarged to 11 mm, and an optical trocar is inserted towards the stomach. The telescope is introduced into the cannula, initiating diagnostic laparoscopy before starting the myomectomy surgery.

The laparoscopic view reveals a large intramural myoma occupying the posterior wall of the uterus, extending to the anterior wall. The myoma measures 13 centimeters and is located on the posterior lateral wall. The right fallopian tube and ovary are visible, along with sections of the intestine.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, with visible anatomical structures including the right ureter showing peristalsis, common and external iliac vessels, and the sacral promontory. The small bowel is lifted to reveal the aorta. The infundibulopelvic ligament is visible above the ureter. Vasopressin mixed with normal saline is injected for hydro dissection. A myoma screw holds the myoma as the surgeon cuts longitudinally into the posterior wall of the uterus.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to extend a longitudinal incision approximately six to seven centimeters on the uterine wall. Muscle layers are spread laterally, and a myoma screw is used to hold the fibroid. Suction irrigation is employed for blunt dissection, gradually enucleating the myoma. A stone holding forceps, a 10 mm instrument, assists in separating the myoma from surrounding tissues, reaching the falciform ligament. Muscle fibers obstructing the myoma's removal are divided with the harmonic scalpel, ensuring the uterine artery is not compromised. The myoma is lifted with the stone holding forceps, avoiding damage to the triangle of doom or cecum, and is fully extracted.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior wall of the uterus, applying a mix of Mishra's knots to approximate the internal muscle layer. Intracorporeal suturing is performed, with the needle being taken out to tie the knots tightly, using a sequence of hitches, winds, and locks to secure the posterior layer.

The surgeon uses a Vandarkar knot pusher to tightly approximate the muscle layer of the uterus with tension sutures, applying Misra's knots for secure extracorporeal suturing. The assistant holds the suture tail to lift it, facilitating the placement of additional tension sutures through the deep muscle and serous layers. Visible old blood is collected in the surgical field, but suction is deferred until after suturing is complete. The procedure transitions to intracorporeal suturing, mixing both techniques for optimal closure.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the posterior wall myoma using a Dhandi jamming knot as a starter suture. The longitudinal incision is securely sutured with multiple layers using number one Vicryl. The assistant holds the tail of the suture to facilitate the process. After completing half of the intracorporeal suturing, it is terminated due to length constraints, and the surgeon resumes suturing with a new suture.

The surgeon applies multiple layers of sutures to repair the uterus after myomectomy, using a combination of extracorporeal and intracorporeal techniques. A deeper suture is placed for the upper layer, followed by continuous suturing with a dandy jamming knot. The final layer involves a full-thickness serous layer suture. The ovary and fallopian tube are visible and protected, with minimal blood loss observed.

The surgeon terminates the suturing process using the tail of the Mishra's knot, ensuring the suture remains tight by pulling with the left hand. The assistant holds the ovarian ligament to maintain tension and prevent loosening, while avoiding the fallopian tube. The final termination is completed using the long tail left from the initial knot.

The surgeon performs the final suturing on the posterior wall of the uterus using a C-reverse C technique. The suture is tightened and then cut, completing the repair. Care is taken to avoid damaging nearby iliac vessels during the process.

The surgeon completes the final knot and cuts the suture, removing the needle through a 5mm port using an endoske needle. The procedure transitions to morselation of the 13 cm myoma, with the electrical morselator directed towards the anterior abdominal wall for safety. The process is performed slowly to ensure precision and safety.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to securely hold the myoma while performing morselation. The morselator is carefully applied to the tissue, ensuring that the myoma is brought close to the device to avoid sliding over it, which could risk damaging nearby structures such as the iliac vessel or small bowel.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic myomectomy, focusing on suturing and morselation of a large myoma. The small intestine is visible nearby, requiring careful morselation to avoid damage. The procedure involves managing blood loss and using vasopressin for hemostasis.

The surgeon continues the morcellation of the myoma, taking care to avoid accidental damage to surrounding structures such as the bowel. The morcellated tissue is being extracted, and the process will continue with the remaining tissue.

The surgeon discusses the ease of removing sub-serious and pedunculated myomas, noting that these types do not require uterine wall repair due to their lack of muscle layer involvement. In contrast, intramural myomas require multiple layers of suturing, presenting a more challenging procedure. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of myoma location and vascularity, highlighting the difficulty of removing smaller myomas situated between the bladder and cervix.

Vascularity also it is always better to have the complete vascular mapping with the MRI or ultrasound because some of the myoma is so highly vascular that it creates problem. Another question which very commonly asked is should we give the GNRH before doing myomectomy? Generally we don't do so because giving GNRH can of course shrink the myoma but it will make the myoma very soft. after that it will be very difficult to apply the myoma screw if you will myoma screw if you will put it it will keep on coming out and it will behave like a boiled potato as you can if you put a screw in the boiled potato it will never hold it will come out so exactly same way if a lot of gnrh is given myoma shrinks in the size it may be useful for open surgery because in open surgery this is your beautiful finger Which is acting as for the enucleation.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to hold the myoma while performing morselation. Fragmented tissue is visible, and a stone-holding forceps is used to remove small pieces scattered around the surgical field. Final irrigation and suction are performed to clear the area.

The surgeon uses suction to remove collected blood from the surgical field. A drain is placed into the posterior cul-de-sac to manage fluid accumulation. The patient's head is positioned upwards, causing any remaining blood in the upper abdomen to move towards the lower abdomen.

The surgeon performs final suction near Morrison's pouch and other areas, ensuring thorough removal of fluids. The 15mm port used for the morcellator is closed to prevent hernia formation, marking the completion of the surgery.
